coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of strongly stabilized yellow fluorescent carbon dots for rapid Golgi apparatus targeting and imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hao
Zhou, Zihao
Ni, Chuyao
Xiao, Jianwu
Cao, Jun
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: Targeted monitoring of the Golgi apparatus (GA) morphology and structure as well as the Golgi apparatus stress response is both essential and challenging. Golgi-targeting phenylsulfonamide-rich carbon dots (PR-CDs) with excitation-dependent, lower cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility were synthesized by one-step solvothermal process. Phenylsulfonamide unit which had a strong binding affinity for the inducible enzyme (cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2)) expressed and accumulated on the Golgi apparatus surface was introduced as a Golgi-targeting moiety. PR-CDs had abundant surface functional groups (amino group, phenylsulfonamide and sulfonic acid group) and excellent fluorescence stability in complex environments (PH, amino acid, ionic liquid, polarity and viscosity). Rapid and accurate imaging of the Golgi apparatus in living cells was achieved by PR-CDs within 15 min and yielded an average Pearson's coefficient of 0.92. Additionally, efficient tracking and imaging of the Golgi apparatus during apoptosis cell death were also accomplished. The successful preparation of PR-CDs provides an innovative subcellular targeting strategy and the prepared PR-CDs could serve as excellent carriers for targeted drug delivery in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow fluorescent carbon dots
Fluorescence stability
Golgi imaging
Golgi dynamic tracking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of the fluorescence properties of PR-CDs
Rapid imaging of the Golgi apparatus
Cytotoxicity of PR-CDs and organelle co-localization

Ltd (\u201CApplication of strong fluorescence graphene quantum dots in heavy metal ion detection and cell imaging\u201D, No. 2022330001004838, Internal No. 22060862-J).

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51603187 ) and the cooperative project funding of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University with Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination Co., Ltd (\u201CApplication of strong fluorescence graphene quantum dots in heavy metal ion detection and cell imaging\u201D, No. 2022330001004838 , Internal No. 22060862-J ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PR-CDs, (b) particle size analysis of PR-CDs, (c) XRD patterns of PR-CDs, (d) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectral of PR-CDs, sulfanilamide and 3-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, (e) XPS survey spectrum of PR-CDs, (f)–(i) XPS fitted spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p, respectively.

(a) Normalized excitation and emission spectra of PR-CDs, (b) UV absorption spectrum of PR-CDs (inset was the photographs of PR-CDs solution under sunlight(left) and UV irradiation (right)), (c) Fluorescence spectra of PR-CDs excited by 410 nm ∼ 510 nm, (d) Colour coordinate of PR-CDs, (e) 3D PL spectra of PR-CDs, (f) Fluorescence lifetime map of PR-CDs.

Fluorescence intensity change of 100 μg/mL PR-CDs in various environments (excitation wavelength: 460 nm): (a) after 7 days of storage, (b) Effect of different Na+ ion concentrations, (c) Effect of various amino acid, (d) Effect of the volume ratio of glycerol to water, (e) Effect of the volume ratio of 1,4-dioxane and water, (f) Effect of varying pH values, (g) Effect of different metal ions and anions, (F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of PR-CDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL in aqueous solution, while F represented the fluorescence intensity of PR-CDs in complex environments.

Fluorescence co-localization experiments of PR-CDs with (A) commercial probes Golgi-Tracker Green II, (B) Hoechst 33342, and (C) Lyso-Tracker Green in H1975 cells. A4-C4: Fluorescence intensity correlation scatter plots of green (Golgi-Tracker Green II), blue (H33342) or Green (Lyso-Tracker) and yellow (PR-CDs) channels generated by ImageJ software. A5-C5: distribution of fluorescence intensity at the positions marked by the white line. Scale bar: 20 μm.

The confocal fluorescent images of H1975 cells co-incubated with the commercial probe Golgi-Tracker Green II for 1 h and PR-CDs for 5-15 min and Fluorescence intensity correlation scatter plots of the green (Golgi-Tracker Green II) and yellow (PR-CDs) channels generated using ImageJ software at different time points. (A)5 min, (B)10 min, (C)15 min. Scale bar: 20 μm.

Confocal microscopy imaging of PR-CDs (yellow) and H33342 (blue) Co-labeled H1975 cells, (a-f) images were acquired after treatment with H2O2 to induce apoptosis via oxidative stress for 0–25 minutes, scale bar: 20 μm.

Synthesis of PR-CDs, their application in Golgi-targeted imaging and apoptosis monitoring, binding mechanism of phenylsulfonamide unit on the surface of carbon dots to COX-2 in the Golgi apparatus matrix

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of strongly stabilized yellow fluorescent carbon dots for rapid Golgi apparatus targeting and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Quantum State Control and Optical Field Manipulation, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 310018 Hangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Quantum State Control and Optical Field Manipulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Quantum State Control and Optical Field Manipulation, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 310018 Hangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination Co. Ltd, Hangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination Co. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination Co. Ltd, Hangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Targeted monitoring of the Golgi apparatus (GA) morphology and structure as well as the Golgi apparatus stress response is both essential and challenging. Golgi-targeting phenylsulfonamide-rich carbon dots (PR-CDs) with excitation-dependent, lower cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility were synthesized by one-step solvothermal process. Phenylsulfonamide unit which had a strong binding affinity for the inducible enzyme (cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2)) expressed and accumulated on the Golgi apparatus surface was introduced as a Golgi-targeting moiety. PR-CDs had abundant surface functional groups (amino group, phenylsulfonamide and sulfonic acid group) and excellent fluorescence stability in complex environments (PH, amino acid, ionic liquid, polarity and viscosity). Rapid and accurate imaging of the Golgi apparatus in living cells was achieved by PR-CDs within 15 min and yielded an average Pearson's coefficient of 0.92. Additionally, efficient tracking and imaging of the Golgi apparatus during apoptosis cell death were also accomplished. The successful preparation of PR-CDs provides an innovative subcellular targeting strategy and the prepared PR-CDs could serve as excellent carriers for targeted drug delivery in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The Golgi apparatus, a vital organelle present in the majority of eukaryotic cells, is crucial for ensuring the proper delivery of proteins, lipids, and various cellular components inside or outside the cell [1]. Golgi apparatus abnormalities lead to diseases, including neurodegenerative diseases, cancer and several autoimmune disorders [2–4]. When cancer cells appear, they overproduce proteins and proteases, leading to an increased need for protein secretion. This elevated demand has enlarged the Golgi apparatus, causing it to swell and shedding is observed in diverse cancers. Since Golgi apparatus morphology detection helps determine whether a cell is normal or cancerous, targeted monitoring of the Golgi apparatus morphology and structure is essential and challenging.

The stress response of the Golgi apparatus was also a critical physiological process. When the pressure signal exceeded the threshold, cell repair and apoptosis in Golgi apparatus were initiated concurrently. Following initiating apoptosis process, the cancer cells ultimately died from overstress [5,6]. This physiological process might have offered new directions for identifying drug targets in tumor therapy. Therefore, it was essential to identify suitable biomarkers for studying the Golgi apparatus stress response and developing new tumor therapies [7].

Various organic small molecule dyes have been widely available. However, they often suffered from drawbacks in cellular imaging, including photobleaching and time-consuming staining procedures [8]. Among the reported probe types, incorporating specific targeting vectors into small molecule probes had been the most efficient and reliable strategy. Ceramides were widely employed for Golgi apparatus targeting and served as key components in commercial Golgi stains. However, the wider application had been limited because the probes known at that time, such as NBD ceramide and BODIPY ceramide, not only required complex synthesis steps and a short residence time [9], but also exhibited long-term toxicity, which could induce cell rupture and death only after 2 h of being co-cultured with cells at low concentrations [8,10]. Therefore, a Golgi-apparatus targeting fluorescent probe capable of imaging and tracking cells needed to be designed and developed to be fast, efficient, economical, environmentally benign, biocompatible.

Researchers were intrigued by the small size and remarkable fluorescent emission properties of carbon dots (CDs) [11]. The ease of surface functionalization, chemical and photostability, and high biocompatibility [12–14] had generated significant interest in clinical and biological applications [15–19]. Functional groups on active sites of the carbon dots' surface imparted unique properties to fluorescent CDs, similar to those of functional ligands [17,20–22]. Researchers have functionalized carbon dots by attaching cysteine or cysteine residues to their surface. Cysteine was recognized as a Golgi-targeting unit, with cysteine residues or cysteine-rich domains localizing to galactosyltransferases and protein kinase D on the Golgi apparatus. However, the high hydrophilicity of cysteines limited their membrane permeability, while their low lipid solubility reduced their suitability as fluorescent probes and targeting carriers in nanomaterial applications [10,23–25]. Recent research revealed that the phenylsulfonamide unit was used as the Golgi apparatus targeting group because of its strong binding affinity for cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2). COX-2 accumulates in the Golgi apparatus, not only correlates closely with tumorigenesis but also participates in Golgi structural remodeling. The COX-2 protein contains multiple functional amino acid residues with potential targeting specificity, making it a promising molecular marker for the design of Golgi-targeted probes [26–28]. Therefore, by using the binding strategy of ligand and receptor, the organelle-targeting functional groups were fixed on the surface of carbon dots, and nanomaterials with excellent membrane permeability and rich surface functional groups were prepared, thereby realizing the efficient imaging and tracking of organelles.

In this paper, based on the ligand-receptor binding strategy, yellow fluorescent carbon dots (PR-CDs) with low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence stability, and rapid and accurate Golgi targeting were synthesized via one-step solvothermal method using 3-Aminobenzenesulfonic acid and phenylsulfonamide as precursors. As shown in Scheme 1 , the enrichment of the phenylsulfonamide unit on the surface of PR-CDs enabled attraction and binding to COX-2 which expressed and accumulated on the surface of Golgi apparatus. PR-CDs achieved precise targeting imaging of Golgi apparatus in living cells, with the Pearson correlation coefficient reaching 0.94. Moreover, the localization time of PR-CDs targeting to the Golgi apparatus was only 15 min, highlighting their rapid response and high efficiency in cellular imaging. Furthermore, PR-CDs were used for visual tracking of Golgi apparatus changes during apoptosis for 25 min through fluorescence imaging. The prepared PR-CDs not only provided a new tool for the early diagnosis of Golgi-related diseases, but also demonstrated their broad application prospects as a new nanomaterial in the fields of drug delivery, pathological monitoring, and treatment.

The preparation method was outlined in Scheme 1. 0.5 g 3-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and 0.7 g sulfanilamide in 25 mL of methanol were sonicated for 8 min. The final solution was then poured into a PTFE liner of autoclave and was heated at 200 °C under high-pressure conditions for 6 h. After cooling to 26 °C, the reaction mixture was obtained and filtered to remove larger particles. Before dialysis, the 500 Da dialysis bag was boiled in ultrapure water for 10 min. Afterward, the filtrate was added to the dialysis bag, which was placed in a 500 mL beaker and dialyzed for 48 h using ultrapure water as the solvent. A 0.22 μm needle filter was used to remove agglomerates from the solution. The filtrate was collected and freeze-dried to obtain PR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the fluorescence properties of PR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: PR-CDs were diluted in ultrapure water to achieve 6 mg/mL solution. Then, 50 μL of the 6 mg/mL PR-CDs solution was added to 2.95 mL water to achieve a 100 μg/mL PR-CDs solution for spectroscopic testing. 2 mL aliquots of 100 μg/mL PR-CDs solution were dispensed into centrifuge tubes for the next fluorescence stability test in different environments. The PR-CDs' fluorescence intensity was recorded as F0 excited by 460 nm visible light. All measurements were performed under identical conditions to ensure consistency.

2 mL 100 μg/mL PR-CDs aqueous solutions were stored in centrifuge tubes at room temperature for different times (from day 0 to day 7). The PR-CDs' fluorescence intensity was measured to test seven-day fluorescence stability.

Na+ aqueous solutions with concentration gradients of (0, 1.05, 2.1, 3.15, 4.2, 5.25, 6.3) M were prepared. 100 μL Na+ solution at different concentrations was added to each 2 mL 100 μg/mL PR-CDs aqueous solution stored in centrifuge tubes and mixed gently. PR-CDs solutions with Na+ concentrations of (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3) M were then prepared. After 10 min of incubation, the effect of Na+ concentration on fluorescence intensity was tested.

0.5 mM ionic aqueous solutions (Zr4+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, K+, Gd3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, CO3 2−, F−, HCO3 −, HPO4 2−, S2−, Cl−, ClO−, NO3 −, SO4 2−) were prepared. Amino acid aqueous solutions (Cys, Gly, GSH, Val, Lys, Leu, Ser, Asp, Ala, Glu, Arg, Met) were prepared at a concentration of 0.5 mM. 100 μL of each ionic solution or amino acid solution was added separately to 2 mL 100 μg/mL PR-CDs aqueous solution and gently mixed. After 10 min of incubation, each sample fluorescence intensity was recorded as F.

Fresh aqueous solutions with pH value (1−11) were prepared separately using ultrapure water, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid. Then, 100 μL of each pH solution was added separately to 2 mL 100 μg/mL PR-CDs aqueous solution and gently mixed. After 10 min of incubation, each sample fluorescence intensity was recorded as F.

Glycerol (Gl) was used to simulate the viscous environment, while 1,4-dioxane (Diox) was used to simulate the polar environment. Glycerol or 1,4-dioxane was mixed with ultrapure water at varying volume ratios (9:1, 8:2, 7:3, 6:4, 5:5, 4:6, 3:7, 2:8, 1:9, 0:10) to create gradient solutions for fluorescence stability test. Subsequently, 100 μL of glycerol or 1,4-dioxane aqueous solutions with different concentration gradients was added separately to 2 mL 100 μg/mL PR-CDs aqueous solution and gently mixed. After 10 min, the PR-CDs' fluorescence intensity in the simulated viscous or polar environment was recorded as F.

The H1975 cell suspension, as an imaging model for PR-CDs, was obtained by digesting in 0.25 % trypsin for 3 min. H1975 cell suspension were inoculated on 20 mm confocal dishes at a density of 60,000/dish and cultured in incubator for 12 h until H1975 cells adhered to confocal dishes. Old medium was removed, and 6 mL PBS was used to wash the cells three times. 1 mL of 1640 medium containing PR-CDs was injected into the cell culture dish using micro pipette tips and cultured for another 30 min. Cells were then treated with commercially available Golgi-Tracker Green II, Hoechst 33342, or Lyso-Tracker Green. Afterward, 6 mL PBS was used to wash the cells three times to eliminate residual commercially available dyes and the cells were imaged using an FV1000 laser confocal fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid imaging of the Golgi apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: The H1975 cell line were incubated with Golgi-Tracker Green II. The residual dyes were removed by PBS washing three times. Next, 2 mL 100 μg/mL of PR-CDs in 1640 medium was injected to the cell culture dish, and the cells were cultured for 5, 10, and 15 min, respectively. The cells were then rinsed three times with PBS to remove any PR-CDs and imaged using an FV1000 laser confocal fluorescence microscope. This experiment aimed to investigate the time-dependent targeting ability of PR-CDs to Golgi apparatus.

2 mL 100 μg/mL solution of PR-CDs in 1640 medium was injected to the H1975 cell culture dish and cultured for 15 min. Afterward, the cells were treated with commercially available Hoechst 33342. The cell lines were cleaned twice with PBS to remove PR-CDs and Hoechst 33342. Subsequently, 1 mL of 1640 complete medium was added, and the cells were imaged using laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscope. After the appropriate image area was selected, 200 μL of 500 μM hydrogen peroxide was injected to the cell culture dish to induce oxidative stress-induced apoptosis. The apoptosis process of Golgi apoptosis was then observed and recorded.

The TEM image of PR-CDs (Fig. 1a) revealed that they were uniformly dispersed and had an approximately spherical morphology and had an average particle size of 5.38 ± 1.3 nm (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. S1a) revealed PR-CDs had a lattice fringe spacing of 0.30 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphene. A broad diffraction peak at around 22.5° in PR-CDs' XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) suggested a graphite-like structure [29–31].

As shown in Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) of PR-CDs (Fig. 1d), three strong absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1, 3345 cm−1 and 3251 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bond respectively [32]. The characteristic peak positions of the NH bond located on 3342 cm−1 and 3245 cm−1 were consistent with that of the raw material p-amino-benzenesulfonamide, which indicated that PR-CDs contained the same phenylsulfonamide group as the raw material did. The characteristic peak at 3041 cm−1 were attributed to the CH stretching vibration in the benzene ring. The absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 was resulted from vibrations of the CC bonds in the carbon skeleton of the aromatic rings in CDs [33,34]. The peak for the CN stretching vibration occurred at 1477 cm−1 [35,36]. The characteristic peak at 1311 cm−1 and the peak at 1031 cm−1 which related to the stretching of double and single bonds between sulfur and oxygen confirmed the presence of S=O/S-O groups [34]. The characteristic peak of the SO bond was related to the raw material p-amino-benzenesulfonamide and 3-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, while the characteristic peak of SO was related only to 3-aminobenzenesulfonic acid. The characteristic peak at 1155 cm−1 was assigned as S − C stretching frequencies [34]. The peak observed at 684 cm−1 was resulted from the bending vibrations of the SC bond [37]. The above infrared spectroscopy analysis results revealed that PR-CDs possessed a conjugated carbon core structure and were extensively modified with functional groups, including amino, hydroxyl, sulfonate, and phenylsulfonamide unit. The phenylsulfonamide unit bound to the carbon dots would be efficiently target Golgi apparatus [35].

As shown in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of PR-CDs (Fig. 1e), significant peaks for S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s were observed. Atomic percentages of S, C, N, and O was 6.41 %, 60.5 %, 12.93 %, and 20.16 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum in Fig. 1f showed two energy peaks at 284.8 eV and 286.5 eV. The first peak occurred because of the existence of -C = C/-C–C bonds in aromatic rings, while the second peak was associated with nitrogen atoms bonded to carbon (C-N/C=N) and sulfur atoms (CS) [35]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 1g exhibited two peaks at 399.2 eV and 401.5 eV. The first peak corresponded to amino nitrogen, while the second peak corresponded to the SN and CN group [34,35]. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 1h showed two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.4 eV, corresponding to SO and SO, respectively [38–40]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum in Fig. 1i could be deconvoluted into peaks at 167.6 eV and 168.7 eV, which were attributed to C-S/S-N and S=O/S-O [41]. The XPS results also reconfirmed that PR-CDs were enriched with phenylsulfonamide unit on their surface, making them suitable for Golgi-targeting and application in Golgi apparatus fluorescence imaging in cells.

The normalized excitation and emission spectra were displayed in Fig. 2a. PR-CDs emitted 560 nm fluorescence excited at the optimal excitation of 460 nm. Three absorption peaks (278 nm, 350 nm and 460 nm) were observed in Fig. 2b. The broad peak at 278 nm was associated with the π-π* transition of aromatic CC [42,43]. The bandgap transition centered at 350 nm resulted from the trapping of excited-state energy in the surface states [44–46]. The absorption band near 460 nm in the visible region was characteristic of an aromatic structure, indicating the presence of a large conjugated system (i.e. carbon core), which was the primary cause of fluorescence [25]. The photographs of PR-CDs aqueous solution (inset in Fig. 2b) showed brown under sunlight and yellow under UV irradiation.

The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2c) showed PR-CDs displayed distinct excitation wavelength-dependent behavior under excitation at wavelengths of 410 to 510 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence emission peaks gradually red-shifted from 548 nm to 563 nm, and the fluorescence intensity initially increased before declining. The highest fluorescence intensity with an emission peak at 560 nm was recorded under 460 nm excitation. The optimal excitation wavelength aligned with the UV–visible absorption spectrum, suggesting the presence of an extensive conjugated system (carbon core) as the primary source of fluorescence. The redshift observed with increasing excitation wavelength indicated that the broad energy level distribution in PR-CDs which facilitated light absorption and emission across a wide range of wavelengths. This excitation-dependent behavior arose from diverse energy traps or surface states, with surface defects or functional groups introduced by nitrogen/sulfur co-doping which played a key role in fluorescence generation [47]. Additionally, based on the CIE 1931 colour coordinates in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence of PR-CDs was primarily yellow, with a warmer hue and balanced brightness and saturation. The 3D fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 2e also indicated that the highest intensity emission was yellow and excitation-dependent [43]. Fig. 2f showed that PR-CDs had a fluorescence lifetime of 3.19 ns, while Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 demonstrated that PR-CDs had an absolute quantum yield of 18.96 % in ultrapure water and 30.61 % in ethanol. PR-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence in the visible region, and their high quantum yields enhanced their potential as fluorescent probes.

In bioimaging, fluorescent probes have to exhibit photostability, and environmental adaptability. A variety of metal cations (Zr4+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, K+, Gd3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+), anions (CO3 2−, F−, HCO3 −, HPO4 2−, S2−, Cl−, ClO−, NO3 −, SO4 2−), amino acids (Cys, Gly, Gsh, Val, Lys, Leu, Ser, Asp, Ala, Glu, Arg, Met), H2O2, pH, viscosity and polarity gradients were taken into consideration to simulate the intracellular environment and the fluorescence performance of PR-CDs in complex environments were assessed. The PR-CDs' fluorescence intensity remained stable (Fig. 3a), and no precipitate was formed after seven days of storage. No significant fluorescence difference was observed for PR-CDs aqueous solutions under varying Na+ ion concentrations (Fig. 3b). For Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity of PR-CDs remained stable without significant changes in various amino acid environments. This indicated that the PR-CDs had good resistance to the presence of amino acids and were able to maintain reliable fluorescence signals even in complex intracellular environments. The intracellular viscous and polar environments were simulated with glycerol and 1,4-dioxane. Shown in Fig. 3d and Fig. 3e, the PR-CDs' fluorescence intensity remained stable. PR-CDs' fluorescence intensity remained stable across different pH environments, in the presence of various metal cations, anions, and hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 3f-g). Building on the excellent fluorescence stability described above, PR-CDs demonstrated a broad range of potential applications. In Fig. S4b, the fluorescence intensity attenuation curves of PR-CDs (excited at 460 nm) and Golgi-targeting commercial probe (excited at 466 nm) were recorded for 30 min. The experimental results showed that under the same excitation intensity and duration, the fluorescence intensity attenuation of PR-CDs was significantly lower than that of Golgi -targeting commercial probes, demonstrating better photostability. This further demonstrated the potential advantages of PR-CDs in long-term live cell imaging. These versatile properties not only highlighted their adaptability in complex microenvironments but also suggested their suitability for bioimaging. The next phase of this study would focus on the application of PR-CDs in cellular imaging, aiming to assess their imaging performance and targeting capability in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of PR-CDs and organelle co-localization
ce:section.ce:para: For carbon dots to be used in live cell imaging, they have to meet several criteria, including low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Previous work had confirmed the adaptability and fluorescence stability of PR-CDs in complex microenvironments. The PR-CDs' cytotoxicity and biocompatibility were evaluated utilizing the CCK8 prior to their application in live cell fluorescence imaging. The PR-CDs-containing medium with concentration gradients (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175 μg/mL) was poured into cell-adherent 96-well plates and co-cultured with the cells for 12 h in incubator. The cell viability of both cancer cells (4 T1, H1975) and normal cells (L929) remained above 85 % following treatment with PR-CDs for 12 h (Fig. S5a). Furthermore, prolonged co-culture of H1975 cells with PR-CDs for 24, 48, and 72 h demonstrated sustained viability rates exceeding 78 % (Fig. S5b). These results confirm that PR-CDs exhibit minimal cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. This allowed PR-CDs to maintain normal cellular function, ensuring that no significant cellular damage occurred during the imaging process and laying a strong foundation for future applications in biomarkers, cell tracking, and biomedical diagnostics.

In performing cellular imaging of PR-CDs, it was pleasantly surprising to find that XPS analysis showed a high S-element content of 6.41 % in PR-CDs. This high content of the phenylsulfonamide unit may have conferred PR-CDs with excellent targeting ability, enabling them to quickly and precisely localize to the intracellular Golgi apparatus. To confirm the targeting function of PR-CDs towards the Golgi apparatus, fluorescence co-localization experiments were conducted. Fig. 4A confirmed that PR-CDs successfully targeted the Golgi apparatus, as evidenced by the high overlap of fluorescence signals with the commercial Golgi-Tracker Green II dye, yielding an average Pearson co-localization coefficient (PCC) of 0.94. Fig. S6 showed the 3D confocal fluorescence image of PR-CDs co-localized with commercial Golgi-targeting probe in live H1975 cells, which accurately imaged the three-dimensional structure of the Golgi apparatus in living cells. Similarly, Fig. S7 demonstrated that PR-CDs exhibited effective Golgi targeting also in normal L929 cells and cancer cell 4 T1 cells, with PCC of 0.81 and 0.76, respectively. This result indicated that PR-CDs exhibited broad Golgi-targeting capability in various types of cells. While the average PCC of PR-CDs with Lyso Tracker Green (targeting lysosomes) and Hoechst 33342(targeting nuclei) were 0.33 and 0.22, respectively, showing PR-CDs fluorescence co-localization in lysosomes and nuclei ineffective. The results demonstrated that PR-CDs exhibited excellent membrane permeability and Golgi apparatus targeting, which was expected to enable dynamic tracking of the Golgi apparatus during apoptosis.

The dyeing process of H1975 cells by the Golgi-Tracker Green II commercial probe was very complex. Following the instructions, the Golgi-Tracker Green II was injected into the cell culture dish, protected from light and incubated with H1975 cells at 4 °C for 30 min. The cells were then washed twice with PBS to get rid of residual fluorescent dye, followed by the addition of the new medium and further incubation at 37 °C for 30 min. The whole process took an hour. The dyeing process of H1975 cell by PR-CDs was fast and convenient. H1975 cells were incubated with medium containing PR-CDs for different time periods (5, 10, and 15 min). As shown in Fig. 5 , the PCC had already reached 0.789 for 5 min and increased to 0.916 for 15 min. The targeting effect of PR-CDs on the Golgi apparatus strengthened as incubation time increased. PR-CDs exhibited fast and efficient targeting ability towards the Golgi apparatus, mainly attributed to their positive charge properties and synergistic effects of surface functional groups. Firstly, the cell membrane surface carried a negative charge, while PR-CDs had a positive charge of approximately 15.2 mV (Fig. S4a), which laid the foundation for the rapid entry of PR-CDs into the cell. Secondly, the surface of PR-CDs abundant basic ligand -NH2 and phenylsulfonamide groups, which enabled them to effectively recognize and target the weakly acidic environment where the Golgi apparatus and COX-2 were located after entering the cell. Especially, the phenylsulfonamide group could bind to COX-2 through hydrogen bonding, further improving the localization accuracy of PR-CDs in Golgi apparatus and achieving rapid and accurate targeted imaging of Golgi apparatus [48]. In comparison to the commercial probe Golgi-Tracker Green II, which required 1 h for positioning, PR-CDs enabled high-quality Golgi imaging in just 15 min, showcasing notable speed advantages and superior imaging performance, further emphasizing their potential as an efficient cell imaging probe.

In the stability experiment shown in Fig. 3g, it was surprising to observe that PR-CDs had maintained strong fluorescence intensity and stability in a hydrogen peroxide environment. Based on this, PR-CDs could be used to detect programmed cell death (apoptosis). After stimulation with hydrogen peroxide-induced oxidative stress, typical apoptosis processes, such as cellular vesiculation, karyorrhexis, and complete disassembly of the Golgi complex, could be observed [49–51]. To visualize the apoptosis processes, H1975 cells were treated with hydrogen peroxide, co-labeled with PR-CDs(yellow) and H33342(blue), and imaged using confocal microscopy. Before the hydrogen peroxide treatment (at t = 0 min), Golgi bodies near the nucleus were clearly visible, displaying intact morphology and a normal distribution. After H1975 cells were treated with hydrogen peroxide for 5 min, the nucleus exhibited slight inward contraction, while the peripheral Golgi bodies were affected, partially cleaved, and redistributed towards the cytoplasm and nucleus. The intensity of this change increased over the next 15 min. By t = 20 min, the nucleus collapsed and began fragmenting, while a small number of incompletely cleaved Golgi bodies remained dispersed in the cell. Eventually, at t = 25 min, the Golgi was completely cleaved and internalized into the nuclear region of the cell, and the nucleus was also completely cleaved, indicating that apoptosis was essentially complete. At this stage, typical apoptosis features, such as nuclear fragmentation and dispersed Golgi fragments, were observed (Fig. 6f). These experiments clearly demonstrated the dynamic alterations in the Golgi apparatus and nucleus during apoptosis induced by hydrogen peroxide-oxidative stress. These results indicated that PR-CDs could effectively label and monitor organelle changes during apoptosis, providing an important imaging tool for in-depth studies of apoptosis mechanisms. These findings demonstrate that PR-CDs exhibit strong potential for in vivo tracking applications across multiple animal models, such as zebrafish and mice [52,53], as evidenced by their excellent biocompatibility, photostability and highly specific targeting to the Golgi apparatus.

In summary, yellow-emitting carbon dots (PR-CDs) containing rich phenylsulfonamide unit with Golgi targeting ability was successfully synthesized through one-step solvothermal method. The PR-CDs, with surfaces rich in functional groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, and phenylsulfonamide units), emitted bright fluorescence at 560 nm. They also exhibited excellent photostability under various microenvironments, including in the presence of different ions, amino acids, varying polarities, and viscosities. Due to the strong binding of the phenylsulfonamide unit to COX-2, which is expressed and accumulated on the Golgi apparatus surface, PR-CDs selectively targeted the Golgi apparatus in cells. PR-CDs enabled rapid imaging and tracking of the Golgi apparatus in live cells within 15 min, achieving a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92. PR-CDs could also be used to effectively label and monitor Golgi apparatus changes during apoptosis. The exploration of this phenylsulfonamide group-containing carbon dots for Golgi apparatus targeting provided an innovative subcellular targeting strategy for morphological monitoring and could be applied to in vivo tracking in the future to explore its application in tracking imaging and drug delivery.

Hao Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Zihao Zhou: Investigation, Data curation. Chuyao Ni: Investigation, Data curation. Jianwu Xiao: Validation. Jun Cao: Validation. Jiaqi Pan: Validation. Chaorong Li: Validation. Yingying Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
